linter_checks.description: Test function contains no pytest assertions.
Assertion is always true, such as assert True or assert X or True.
Only assertion checks returncode == 0.
Assertion allows non-zero exits with returncode in a list.
Test body is just pass.
Assertion uses A or B instead of asserting an exact outcome.
If branch asserts with no else, silently passing when false.
Both if and else branches assert, suggesting non-deterministic behavior.
Except handler with pass swallows failures.
All assertions only check returncode, len, or isdigit.
Substring check is shorter than 15 characters.
Test writes to its own golden file.
Golden file is referenced in docstring but never compared with ==.
Golden file is mentioned in docstring but not found in the test body.
Loop-only assertions have no pre-loop length check.
Sole assertion is len(x) > N.
Assertion uses len(x) >= N, a relative bound that verifies no content.
any()/all() assertion has no non-empty guard.
path.exists() assertion has no content assertion.
All assertions are negative checks such as not in or !=.
Missing or too-short CATCHES docstring.

checks.evidence: No forbidden workspace/source strings in pytest wrappers.
Test wrappers avoid direct subprocess/source inspection and route behavior through run_lua.
1306 generated cases use -e with explicit stdout/stderr/returncode and no path/env leaks.
No local workspace/gold/_src absolute path leaks in hidden fixture files.
6 native fixture files exist but pytest wrappers do not compile or name them.
The upstream suite is invoked with _U=true and all.lua disables internal T/C API checks.
28 upstream Lua scripts are passed to the executable under cwd, not used to inspect candidate source.
USAGE.md names the CLI, language, runtime, and standard-library behavior families covered by hidden tests.
USAGE.md explains black-box CLI observation and forbids binary/source shortcuts.
USAGE.md states that hidden scoring runs only the compiled executable and requires a self-contained runtime.

Cleanroom image root-owns the oracle, grants execute-only permission, preserves it during development builds via ./candidate, strips prebuilt executables from submissions, and solve runs with network disabled.

Lua mini-SWE runner applies token/reasoning/observation caps, forces the named bash tool by default, strips Gemini thought metadata, removes commit instructions, enforces source-first/build phases, rejects placeholder submissions, and blocks oracle-inspection/scratchpad/git/network/package/system-search/repeated-command stagnation.
Harness sets LC_ALL=C/TZ=UTC and checks suite completion without pinning random seed or timing values.

notes: This deterministic audit proves the hidden tests are black-box executable interactions, not direct candidate-source checks.
It cannot prove that a short agent run will infer the full Lua language and standard library; it checks only that the behavior is observable through docs plus normal oracle execution.

findings.finding: Deterministic hidden-test fairness audit now passes before future solve runs.
The previous 36-call submission embedded and executed the gold oracle; the evaluator now rejects it before scoring.
The latest trajectory tried local package/header/source discovery commands, but no Lua source was found or used. Score the run as failed with a trace warning, not as disqualified.
The scoring container originally lacked explicit network isolation. The evaluator now uses an offline pytest image and reran the latest submission with --network none during scoring.
Wrapper detection catches exact embedded gold bytes and known wrapper strings, but transformed encodings could evade static heuristics. Strict task packaging remains the primary defense.
Solver-facing docs identify Lua 5.5.1 behavior and document the offline compile contract plus end-to-end behavior surface, which is necessary documentation rather than original source leakage.
The latest scored run is a plausible failed reimplementation stub rather than a shortcut: it passed 7 of 1338 behavioral tests.
